front: Philosopher: illustrated the difference between sense and reference using 'Hesperus is Phosphorus' — both names refer to Venus but differ in their 'mode of presentation'
work: On Sense and Reference (Uber Sinn und Bedeutung)

front: Philosopher: Basic Law V was shown inconsistent by Russell's paradox; Russell's letter arrived while the second volume of Basic Laws of Arithmetic was at the printing press
work: Basic Law V and Russell's Paradox

front: Philosopher: introduced the context principle (words only have meaning in the context of a proposition) in a work that defines numbers as extensions of 'equinumerous' concepts
work: The Foundations of Arithmetic (Grundlagen der Arithmetik)

front: Concept: states that Hume's principle is sufficient to derive all Peano axioms in second-order logic; rehabilitated by Crispin Wright and George Boolos
Philosopher: Michael Dummett attributes the beginning of the linguistic turn to this thinker's use of the context principle (paragraph 62 of Foundations of Arithmetic)
work: The Foundations of Arithmetic (Grundlagen der Arithmetik)

front: Work: introduced the turnstile symbol, universal quantifier, conditional, and a unique two-dimensional notation for logic; its preface notes Leibniz underestimated the difficulties of a calculus philosophicus

Concept: a problem for expressivism/non-cognitivism where 'it is wrong to tell lies' seems to mean something different in the conditional 'if it is wrong to tell lies, then it is wrong to get your brother to lie'
work: On Sense and Reference (Uber Sinn und Bedeutung)

front: Philosopher: Susanne Bobzien identified 120 passages where this thinker appeared to plagiarize a Stoic philosophy anthology; Gabriel Nuchelmans found similarity with the concept of lekta
Philosopher: opposed David Hilbert's claim that axioms are definitions in a controversy over geometry; a late fragment calls non-Euclidean geometry a 'pseudoscientific museum piece'
Philosopher: added a last-minute appendix to Basic Laws of Arithmetic after receiving Russell's letter, which turned out to imply the universe of discourse contains exactly one object
work: Basic Law V and Russell's Paradox

front: Philosopher: Nathan Salmon's 'modified naive theory' proposes the information value of a singular term is simply its referent, partly titled for this thinker's puzzle
work: On Sense and Reference (Uber Sinn und Bedeutung)

front: Philosopher: Edward Zalta wrote the oldest article in the Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy about this thinker
Work: includes the Julius Caesar problem (rejected definitions that couldn't determine if Caesar was a number); J.L. Austin translated it; three 'fundamental principles of enquiry'
work: The Foundations of Arithmetic (Grundlagen der Arithmetik)

front: Philosopher: argued that mathematical Platonism is the only defensible view; both Brouwer (intuitionism) and Hilbert (formalism) disagreed with this thinker's logicism
